content.text: A severity and impact matrix for agentic AI incidents

is a structured classification framework that maps the combination of business impact and technical blast radius to a discrete severity level (SEV-1 through SEV-4), each with predefined response time targets, escalation paths, and communication obligations. For agentic AI systems, the matrix must go beyond generic service outage categories to include AI-specific failure modes: runaway agents taking unintended actions, hallucination at scale, LLM provider outages, model-driven financial exposure, and AI-mediated data breaches.

Without a defined severity matrix, every incident becomes a negotiation. On-call engineers must individually decide how urgently to escalate, when to wake up leadership, and whether to invoke the war room — under time pressure, with incomplete information. This inconsistency leads to under-escalation (treating a SEV-1 as a SEV-3) and over-escalation (waking the CTO for a non-critical alert). AI incidents compound this problem because the harm is often non-obvious: an agent silently producing wrong outputs may not generate error codes, and financial damage from runaway tool invocations may not appear in standard SLO metrics.
Removes ambiguity from incident response: on-call engineers apply a decision rule, not a judgment call.
Ensures AI-specific failure modes (hallucination at scale, runaway actions) are treated with appropriate urgency.
Standardises response time targets and escalation paths so all engineers follow the same protocol.
Enables incident metrics (MTTD, MTTR) to be meaningfully compared across incidents of the same severity class.
Provides a clear basis for regulatory notifications — some jurisdictions require breach notification within 72 hours, which requires rapid SEV classification.
Edge cases exist where a scenario falls between severity levels — the matrix must provide a default rule (when uncertain, escalate higher).
AI-specific failures (silent hallucination, partial degradation) may not trigger standard monitoring alerts, making automated severity assignment unreliable.
The matrix must be maintained as the product evolves — a SEV-3 threshold that was appropriate for 10,000 users is too lenient at 10 million.
Business impact assessment during the first minutes of an incident is inherently uncertain — severity may be upgraded as more information emerges.
(the severity of the effect on users, revenue, or regulatory status) and

(the proportion of users affected and the depth of systems involved). The intersection determines the severity level. The on-call engineer applies the matrix by asking two questions: "How bad is the user experience?" and "How many users are affected?" When in doubt, the default is to escalate to the higher severity class.

AI-specific additions to the standard matrix include columns for agent action scope (can the agent perform irreversible actions?), data exposure scope (is personal or confidential data at risk?), and model confidence signal (is the agent degrading silently?). These additions catch AI-specific failure modes that a standard service matrix would classify as SEV-3 when they are actually SEV-1.

attrs.alt: Severity matrix showing 4 quadrants: SEV-1 critical (high impact, high blast radius), SEV-2 high, SEV-3 medium, SEV-4 low, with examples and response targets

content.text: The matrix maps business impact (Y axis) against technical blast radius (X axis) into four severity levels. SEV-1 requires an IC, war room, and executive notification. SEV-2 requires on-call team response and status page update. SEV-3 is handled during business hours. SEV-4 goes to the backlog.
Every time an on-call engineer receives an alert or customer report that requires a response decision.
During post-incident review to validate whether the initial severity classification was correct.
When updating SLO thresholds or alert policies, to ensure the thresholds map to the correct severity levels.
During incident simulation exercises to test whether engineers agree on severity classification for ambiguous scenarios.
Define impact and blast radius scales with objective, measurable thresholds specific to your product (e.g., "high impact = >10% of users cannot complete a primary use case").
Add AI-specific failure modes to each severity row: data exposure scope, agent action scope, hallucination rate.
Define concrete response time targets and escalation paths for each severity level and publish them in the on-call runbook.
Create a classification decision tree (see next topic) that guides engineers through the matrix for ambiguous cases.
Code-review the matrix with representatives from engineering, legal, communications, and customer support — all will respond differently based on severity.
Run quarterly calibration exercises: present engineers with 5-10 ambiguous scenarios and check that their classifications agree with the matrix definition.

# severity-matrix.yaml — machine-readable for alert routing
severities:
  SEV1:
    label: "CRITICAL"
    response_time_minutes: 15
    requires_ic: true
    requires_war_room: true
    executive_notification: true
    status_page_required: true
    examples:
      - data_breach_or_pii_exposure
      - complete_service_outage_gt_50pct_users
      - runaway_agent_with_financial_exposure
      - regulatory_control_failure
    ai_specific:
      - agent_executed_irreversible_actions_without_approval
      - llm_output_caused_measurable_user_harm_at_scale

  SEV2:
    label: "HIGH"
    response_time_minutes: 60
    requires_ic: false  # optional at IC's discretion
    status_page_required: true
    examples:
      - major_feature_degraded_gt_20pct_fail_rate
      - llm_provider_outage_impacting_users
      - tool_integration_failures
    ai_specific:
      - hallucination_rate_gt_threshold_in_production
      - agent_refusing_valid_requests_gt_20pct

  SEV3:
    label: "MEDIUM"
    response_time_hours: 4
    business_hours_only: false
    status_page_required: false
    examples:
      - elevated_error_rate_lt_20pct
      - latency_degradation_p95_gt_slo
      - single_tenant_ai_feature_failure

  SEV4:
    label: "LOW"
    response_time: "next_sprint"
    examples:
      - cosmetic_ai_output_issues
      - non_critical_feature_degradation
      - infrastructure_warnings

# SEV-1 Response Checklist (first 15 minutes)

## Immediate (T+0 to T+5 min)
- [ ] Declare SEV-1 in #incidents Slack channel
- [ ] Page on-call IC (if not already paged)
- [ ] Start bridge call / war room
- [ ] If agent is taking harmful actions: invoke AGENT_STOP_ALL runbook
- [ ] If PII exposed: immediately notify security@company.com

## First 15 minutes (T+5 to T+15 min)
- [ ] IC identified and on bridge
- [ ] Initial status page posted ("Investigating")
- [ ] Exec notification sent (CEO, CTO, CPO as applicable)
- [ ] Legal/compliance notified if PII or regulatory control involved
- [ ] Initial mitigation in progress (rollback? rate-limit? circuit-break?)

## Classification review (T+15 min)
- [ ] Confirm or downgrade severity based on new information
- [ ] Assign communications lead (separate from IC)
- [ ] Set next status page update time (every 30 min for SEV-1)

A travel booking agent starts autonomously cancelling flight reservations due to a mis-parsed cancellation intent. At T+0, the on-call engineer receives a customer complaint. Applying the matrix: "agent executed irreversible actions" → SEV-1, regardless of scale. The engineer declares SEV-1, invokes the AGENT_STOP_ALL runbook to halt all booking agent sessions, and pages the IC within 3 minutes. The IC is on the bridge within 10 minutes. Legal is notified due to the financial and contractual exposure.

Post-incident review reveals the initial severity classification was correct. 14 bookings were cancelled (financial exposure: ~,000). Without the matrix, the engineer might have treated this as a SEV-2 ("feature degradation") and not invoked the stop runbook, allowing more cancellations while investigating the root cause.
Treating AI hallucination incidents as SEV-4 because they don't generate HTTP 5xx errors — hallucination at scale is typically SEV-2.
Not including irreversible agent actions as an automatic SEV-1 trigger — the harm is time-dependent and increases while engineers debate severity.
Using percentage-based thresholds that are appropriate for large user bases but too lenient for small populations where even 1% is hundreds of users.
Allowing downgrade-only policies — incidents should be re-evaluable upward as more information emerges.
Not calibrating the matrix regularly — a matrix written for an early-stage product with 1,000 users is too conservative for a product with 1 million users.
Always include AI-specific triggers (irreversible actions, PII exposure, hallucination rate) as explicit entries in the SEV-1 and SEV-2 columns.
Default upward when uncertain: the cost of a false SEV-1 (unnecessary escalation) is far lower than a false SEV-3 (under-response to a critical incident).
Publish the machine-readable matrix as a YAML file used by both alert routing automation and human runbooks.
Run calibration exercises: present 5 ambiguous scenarios to 5 engineers and verify they agree on severity within one level.
Review and update the matrix after every SEV-1 incident to incorporate learnings about novel failure modes.

As the product scales, percentage-based thresholds remain meaningful, but absolute count thresholds must be adjusted. At 10,000 users, 1 % failure is 100 users; at 10 million users, 1 % is 100,000 users. Consider defining thresholds as the greater of a percentage or an absolute count to maintain appropriate sensitivity across growth stages.

The severity matrix should be accessible during network and observability outages — publish it as a static document in a location independent of the monitoring systems (e.g., a printed quick-reference card, a Confluence page, a GitHub wiki). If engineers can only find the matrix via the internal portal that is also degraded during an incident, the classification process fails exactly when it is most needed.
A severity matrix removes ambiguity from incident response by providing a rule-based classification, not a judgment call.
AI incidents require additional matrix entries: irreversible agent actions, hallucination rate, PII exposure, and financial impact from tool invocations.
When uncertain, default upward — the cost of an unnecessary SEV-1 response is much lower than the cost of an under-response.
Publish the matrix as a machine-readable YAML file used by both alert routing automation and human runbooks.
Run calibration exercises quarterly and update the matrix after every major incident.
Ensure the matrix is accessible offline or without network access — it is most needed when systems are degraded.
